sections.heading: Fixed Mindset vs Growth Mindset
sections.body:
Psychologist Carol Dweck's decades of research at Stanford University identified two fundamental mindsets that shape how we approach challenges, failure, and learning:

Fixed Mindset: The belief that your abilities, intelligence, and talents are fixed traits. You're either smart or you're not. Success proves your innate talent; failure reveals your limitations.

Growth Mindset: The belief that abilities can be developed through dedication, hard work, and learning. Intelligence is like a muscle — it grows with exercise. Failure is feedback, not identity.

sections.heading: The Power of 'Yet'
sections.body:
One of Dweck's most impactful discoveries is the power of the word 'yet.' Fixed mindset says: 'I can't do this.' Growth mindset says: 'I can't do this YET.' This simple linguistic shift transforms a dead-end into a path forward.

Children who were taught 'yet' showed 34% greater resilience when facing difficult math problems. Adults who adopted 'yet' language reported 25% higher persistence in challenging projects.

sections.heading: How Mindset Affects Achievement
sections.body:
In Dweck's studies:
• Students with growth mindset who received a difficult problem chose to work on it 40% longer than fixed-mindset peers.
• Employees with growth mindset sought feedback 3x more frequently.
• Athletes with growth mindset improved performance 40% more after setbacks.
• Leaders with growth mindset built teams with 26% higher energy and 33% greater commitment.

Mindset is not just positive thinking — it's a framework that determines whether you interpret challenges as threats or opportunities.

jargon.definition: The belief that abilities and intelligence can be developed through effort, good strategies, and input from others. Not about being positive — it's about being open to learning.
The belief that abilities are innate and unchangeable. This mindset leads to avoiding challenges, giving up easily, and seeing effort as pointless.
The brain's ability to form new neural connections throughout life. This is the biological basis for growth mindset — intelligence IS improvable.

summary: Carol Dweck's research shows two mindsets: fixed (abilities are static) and growth (abilities develop through effort). Growth mindset leads to greater persistence, learning, and achievement. The biological basis is neuroplasticity — your brain physically changes with practice. Start by noticing your fixed-mindset triggers, then practice adding 'yet' to limitations.
